canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, the concept of persuasiveness is framed in two distinct ways. The more prevalent usage treats it as a quality of generated arguments, defined as their effectiveness at convincing an audience or their "latent capacity... to influence others’ opinions" ("Improving Large Language Models Function Calling and Interpretability via Guided-Structured Templates"). In this context, it is seen as an "indicator of argument quality." A contrasting line of work frames "persuadability" as a model vulnerability, defining it as an LLM's disposition to be influenced by biased or false information, noting that "LLMs are highly persuadable" ("AnchorAttention: Difference-Aware Sparse Attention with Stripe Granularity"). This perspective suggests the problem is not being persuaded, but rather "identifying what not to be persuaded by." When viewed as an argument quality, the construct is operationalized and measured via `Human evaluation` across multiple dimensions. It is also linked to observable "linguistic markers and turn-taking dynamics" as a means of identification.
papers: AnchorAttention: Difference-Aware Sparse Attention with Stripe Granularity
Disambiguation in Conversational Question Answering in the Era ofLLMs and Agents: A Survey
EuroGEST: Investigating gender stereotypes in multilingual language models
Improving Large Language Models Function Calling and Interpretability via Guided-Structured Templates
Not-Just-Scaling Laws: Towards a Better Understanding of the Downstream Impact of Language Model Design Decisions
Threading the Needle: Reweaving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning to Explain Human Label Variation

definitions.text: The extent to which generated arguments are effective at convincing an audience across contexts.
definitions.paper: Disambiguation in Conversational Question Answering in the Era ofLLMs and Agents: A Survey

definitions.text: The disposition of an LLM to be influenced by biased, misleading, or false information presented as factual, leading to adoption of malicious data without explicit instruction.
definitions.paper: AnchorAttention: Difference-Aware Sparse Attention with Stripe Granularity

definitions.text: The latent capacity of an argument or participant to influence others’ opinions, often linked to linguistic markers and turn-taking dynamics.
definitions.paper: Improving Large Language Models Function Calling and Interpretability via Guided-Structured Templates

relationships.paper: Disambiguation in Conversational Question Answering in the Era ofLLMs and Agents: A Survey
relationships.evidence: For a more comprehensive analysis, we conduct a human evaluation... They follow the same five evaluation dimensions used in the automatic evaluation... (Section 4.2)
